questions: You take your time and don't rush things. A project deadline got moved up by a week and your team hasn't started the final deliverable yet. What's your approach?
You take your time and don't rush things. You realize you have thirty minutes to get ready and drive to an appointment that's twenty-five minutes away. How do you handle it?
You take your time and don't rush things. Your manager sends a message asking for an update on something you haven't looked at yet, marked as high priority. How do you respond?
You take your time and don't rush things. You're cooking dinner and realize you forgot a key ingredient, and the stores close in fifteen minutes. What do you do?
You take your time and don't rush things. A client emails saying they need a proposal by end of day or they'll go with a competitor. How do you handle it?
You take your time and don't rush things. You notice a leak under your kitchen sink that's slowly dripping onto the floor. What's your response?
You take your time and don't rush things. You're packing for a flight that leaves in four hours and you haven't started yet. How do you approach it?
You take your time and don't rush things. Your team is in a meeting and a production issue comes in that's affecting customers right now. What do you do?
You take your time and don't rush things. You're studying for an exam that's tomorrow morning and you've only covered half the material. How do you proceed?
You take your time and don't rush things. You get a call from your kid's school saying they're sick and need to be picked up, but you're in the middle of something at work. How do you react?
